Step 1: Calculate the total cost price of the sugar oranges.
The supermarket purchases 500 kg of sugar oranges at a cost price of 4.80 yuan per kilogram.
\[
\text{Total cost price} = 500 \times 4.80 = 2400 \text{ yuan}
\]

Step 2: Determine the expected weight after loss.
The expected weight loss is 10%, so the remaining weight is 90% of the original weight.
\[
\text{Remaining weight} = 500 \times 0.90 = 450 \text{ kg}
\]

Step 3: Calculate the target total revenue to achieve a 20% profit.
A 20% profit on the cost price means the total revenue should be 120% of the cost price.
\[
\text{Target total revenue} = 2400 \times 1.20 = 2880 \text{ yuan}
\]

Step 4: Determine the retail price per kilogram.
To achieve the target total revenue with the remaining weight of 450 kg, the retail price per kilogram should be:
\[
\text{Retail price per kilogram} = \frac{2880}{450} = 6.40 \text{ yuan}
\]

Therefore, the retail price per kilogram should be \boxed{6.40} yuan.

Step 1: Calculate the total cost of the sugar oranges.
The supermarket purchases 500 kg of sugar oranges at a cost price of 4.80 yuan per kilogram.
\[
\text{Total cost} = 500 \times 4.80 = 2400 \text{ yuan}
\]

Step 2: Determine the expected weight after the loss.
The expected weight loss is 10%, so the expected weight after the loss is:
\[
\text{Expected weight} = 500 \times (1 - 0.10) = 500 \times 0.90 = 450 \text{ kg}
\]

Step 3: Calculate the target revenue to achieve a 20% profit.
A 20% profit on the cost price means the revenue should be:
\[
\text{Target revenue} = 2400 \times (1 + 0.20) = 2400 \times 1.20 = 2880 \text{ yuan}
\]

Step 4: Determine the retail price per kilogram.
The retail price per kilogram should be the target revenue divided by the expected weight:
\[
\text{retail price per kilogram} = \frac{2880}{450} = 6.40 \text{ yuan}
\]

The retail price per kilogram should be \(\boxed{6.40}\).
